summary: Gruber highlights a significant usability problem with the iPhone X: users don't understand they need to double-click the physical side button to confirm Apple Pay and App Store purchases. He documents multiple reports from Hacker News commenters, family members, and tech journalists who were confused by the on-screen animation pointing to an off-screen button. He explains the security rationale — a physical button press can't be faked by a malicious app — but acknowledges the fundamental tension that iPhone users expect all interactions to happen on screen. He notes this isn't entirely new, as Touch ID had a similar problem with users touching the on-screen fingerprint icon instead of the home button.
key_points: Multiple technically-savvy users were confused by the iPhone X's 'Double Click to Pay' animation, not realizing it referred to the physical side button
Apple requires the physical side button for payments because it can't be spoofed by apps — only Apple's own software can override the side button's default behavior
The fundamental design tension is that the screen IS the phone, so users don't expect to perform actions off-screen, especially on the bezel-minimized iPhone X
This confusion isn't unique to Face ID — Touch ID had an analogous problem with users pressing the on-screen fingerprint icon instead of the home button
Gruber suggests Apple needs a more explicit first-time indication that the hardware button is required
money_quotes: The reason why Apple requires you to press the physical side button to confirm a purchase with Apple Pay or in the App Store is because pressing the side button can't be faked by an app.
People naturally expect everything they do on an iPhone to be done on screen. The screen is the phone — and that's even more true with the iPhone X.
Even with an animation pointing to the side button on screen, it doesn't occur to people that they need to do something off-screen to authorize the transaction.
Double clicking the side button to confirm a purchase effectively guarantees that it was a legitimate payment experience.
They think the affordance on the side of the screen is the button they're supposed to double tap (and they don't notice the verbal distinction between 'click' and 'tap').
I suggest: 'Press the damn side button twice. It's on the damn right edge of the phone.'
key_insight: The iPhone X's physical side button for payment confirmation is a case where security requirements directly conflict with the user expectation that all interactions happen on screen, and Apple hasn't adequately bridged that gap.
